analogy: Think of "Physical and Chemical Changes" through the chapter's own opening images: Respiration in Organisms 6 O ne day Boojho was eagerly waiting to meet his grandparents who were coming to the town after a year. He was in a real hurry as he wanted to receive them at the bus-stop. He ran fast and reached the... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 6.1 WHY DO WE RESPIRE?, SCIENCE 58, 6.2 BREATHING, CAUTION. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Physical and Chemical Changes is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that respiration in Organisms 6 O ne day Boojho was eagerly waiting to meet his grandparents who were coming to the town after a year.

The text explains that he was in a real hurry as he wanted to receive them at the bus-stop.

The text explains that he ran fast and reached the bus-stop in a few minutes.

The text explains that his grandmother asked him why he was breathing so fast.

The text explains that boojho told her that he came running all the way.

The text explains that he wondered why running makes a person breathe faster.

The text explains that the answer to Boojho’s question lies in understanding why we breathe.

The text explains that in Chapter 2 you learnt that all organisms are made of small microscopic units called cells.

The text explains that a cell is the smallest structural and functional unit of an organism.

The text explains that each cell of an organism performs certain functions such as nutrition, transport, excretion and reproduction.

The text explains that to perform these functions, the cell needs energy.

The text explains that even when we are eating, sleeping or reading we require energy.

The text explains that can you say why your parents insist that you should eat regularly?

The text explains that the food has stored energy, which is released during respiration.

The text explains that therefore, all living organisms respire to get energy from food.

The text explains that we breathe out air which is rich in carbon dioxide.

The text explains that the process of breakdown of food in the cell with the release of energy is called cellular respiration.

The text explains that they respire anaerobically and during this process yield alcohol.

The text explains that does this explain w h y do w e f e e l h u n g r y a f t e r a physical activity?

The text explains that everyone was boasting that she/he Fig 6.4 Human respiratory system The air around us has various types of unwanted particles, such as smoke, dust, pollens, etc.

The text explains that can you explain this change on the basis of what you learnt in Chapter 5?

The text explains that now, you should be able to explain the mechanism of breathing.

The text explains that sCIENCE 64 Fig.6.9 Tracheal system Tracheae 6.5 B REATHING IN O THER A NIMALS Animals such as elephants, lions, cows, goats, frogs, lizards, snakes, birds, have lungs in their chest cavities like the human beings.

The text explains that boojho wants to know if cockroaches, snails, fish, earthworms, ants and mosquitoes also have lungs.

The text explains that inhaled air Exhaled air Lungs 21% oxygen 16.4% oxygen 4.4% carbon dioxide 0.04% carbon dioxide Straw Lime water Earthworm: Recall from Chapter 6 of Class VI that earthworms breathe through their skins.

The text explains that the skin of an earthworm feels moist and slimy on touching.

The text explains that you have already learnt in Chapter 1 that the leaves of the plants have tiny pores called stomata for exchange of oxygen and carbon dioxide.

The text explains that in this chapter you learnt that respiration is a vital biological What you have learnt  Respiration is essential for survival of living organisms.

The text explains that energy is released in the process.  The breakdown of glucose occurs in the cells of an organism (cellular respiration).  If the food is broken down with the use of oxygen, it is called aerobic respiration.

The text explains that if the breakdown occurs without the use of oxygen, the respiration is called anaerobic respiration.  During heavy exercise when the supply of oxygen to our muscle cells is insufficient, food breakdown is by anaerobic respiration.  Breathing is a part of the process of respiration during which an organism takes in the oxygen-rich air and gives out air rich in carbon dioxide.

The text explains that the respiratory organs for the exchange of gases vary in different organisms.  During inhalation, our lungs expand and then come back to the original state as the air moves out during exhalation.  Increased physical activity enhances the rate of breathing.  In animals like cow, buffalo, dog and cat the respiratory organs and the process of breathing are similar to those in humans.  In earthworm, the exchange of gases occurs through the moist skin.

The text explains that the breakdown of glucose in the plant cells is similar to that in other living beings. process.

The text explains that match the items in Column I with those in Column II: Column I Column II (a) Yeast (i) Earthworm (b) Diaphragm (ii) Gills (c) Skin (iii) Alcohol (d) Leaves (iv) Chest cavity (e) Fish (v) Stomata (f) Frog (vi) Lungs and skin (vii) Tracheae

The text explains that these words may be present in any direction — upwards, downwards, or along the diagonals.

The text explains that s V M P L U N G S C Z G Q W X N T L R M A T I D O T C I Y R X Y M S R A B R H I A N T A Y S T P T B Z R C E M I A M T S I H A S P I R A C L E S N E D K J N S A T (i) The air tubes of insects (ii) Skeletal structures surrounding chest cavity (iii) Muscular floor of chest cavity (iv) Tiny pores on the surface of leaf (v) Small openings on the sides of the body of an insect (vi) The respiratory organs of human beings (vii) The openings through which we inhale (viii) An anaerobic organism (ix) An organism with tracheal system 9.

The text explains that (c) The temperature of air is higher than that on the ground.

The text explains that (d) The pressure of air is higher than that on the ground.

The text explains that you will find flap like structures on both sides of their heads.

The text explains that on the basis of these observations, explain the process of respiration in the fish.

The text explains that you can also collect material on this topic from other sources.

The text explains that if there is a difference in any of these cases, try to find the reason.

The text explains that you can seek help of your teacher or parents.

The text explains that find out the percentage of people of your area who smoke.

The text explains that if you have a smoker in your family, confront him with the material that you have collected.

The text explains that ask the doctor: (a) When does a person need artificial respiration?

The text explains that (b) Does the person need to be kept on artificial respiration temporarily or permanently?

The text explains that (c) From where can the person get supply of oxygen for artificial respiration?

The text explains that measure the breathing rate of the members of your family and some of your friends.

The text explains that investigate: (c) If the breathing rate of children is different from that of adults.

The text explains that (d) If the breathing rate of males is different from that of females.

Source: NCERT Class 7 Science, Chapter 6: Physical and Chemical Changes

His grandmother asked him why he was breathing so fast.

prelims_traps: Do not treat 6.1 WHY DO WE RESPIRE? and SCIENCE 58 as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Physical and Chemical Changes; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Physical and Chemical Changes through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 6.1 WHY DO WE RESPIRE? and SCIENCE 58 to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.

mcqs.question_text: In the NCERT discussion of Physical and Chemical Changes, which statement is supported by the source text about 6.1 WHY DO WE RESPIRE??
options: Respiration in Organisms 6 O ne day Boojho was eagerly waiting to meet his grandparents who were coming to the town after a year.
He was in a real hurry as he wanted to receive them at the bus-stop.
His grandmother asked him why he was breathing so fast.
The answer to Boojho’s question lies in understanding why we breathe.

mcqs.question_text: In the NCERT discussion of Physical and Chemical Changes, which statement is supported by the source text about CAUTION?
options: In the cells, oxygen in the air helps in the breakdown of food.
The process of breakdown of food in the cell with the release of energy is called cellular respiration.
Cellular respiration takes place in the cells of all organisms.
In the cell, the food (glucose) is broken down into carbon dioxide and water using oxygen.
